coredata.dc:description: As a novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots are restricted by their poor fluorescence in the solid state, although they exhibit favorable photoluminescence in solution. N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and solid-state fluorescence films were prepared using green and renewable cellulose-derived materials, respectively. The hydrogen bonding network of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) inhibits the self-aggregation behavior of N-CDs, which leads to solid-state fluorescence. The N-CDs was initially obtained with CMC as the carbon source, which showed excellent blue fluorescence. Subsequently, the white-emitting films (N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC) were successfully constructed by combining the blue fluorescent N-CDs with the red fluorescence of the europium metal-organic framework. The prepared films showed stable luminescence within 30 days and in the heat environment at 120 °C for 3 h. After covering the N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films on the UV-LED chip with ultraviolet emissive at 365 nm, the white light-emitting diodes were obtained, which exhibited excellent color characteristics with the color coordinates, a correlated color temperature, and a color rendering index of (0.31, 0.32), 6580 K, and 92, respectively. The strategy proposed in this work will provide ideas for generating optical luminescent films from biomass and provide guidance for solid-state fluorescence biomass materials.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots are restricted by their poor fluorescence in the solid state, although they exhibit favorable photoluminescence in solution. N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and solid-state fluorescence films were prepared using green and renewable cellulose-derived materials, respectively. The hydrogen bonding network of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) inhibits the self-aggregation behavior of N-CDs, which leads to solid-state fluorescence. The N-CDs was initially obtained with CMC as the carbon source, which showed excellent blue fluorescence. Subsequently, the white-emitting films (N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC) were successfully constructed by combining the blue fluorescent N-CDs with the red fluorescence of the europium metal-organic framework. The prepared films showed stable luminescence within 30 days and in the heat environment at 120 °C for 3 h. After covering the N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films on the UV-LED chip with ultraviolet emissive at 365 nm, the white light-emitting diodes were obtained, which exhibited excellent color characteristics with the color coordinates, a correlated color temperature, and a color rendering index of (0.31, 0.32), 6580 K, and 92, respectively. The strategy proposed in this work will provide ideas for generating optical luminescent films from biomass and provide guidance for solid-state fluorescence biomass materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have caught various focus owing to their unique physicochemical and excellent photoluminescent properties, low biotoxicity, and superior resistance to photo-bleaching, which can be used in the fields of bio-imaging technology [1,2], chemical analysis [3], catalyst [4], optoelectronic devices [5], secondary energy utilization [6] and sensor detection [7–9]. The selection of low-cost, easily accessible, environmentally friendly and renewable carbon sources is a feasible way to realize CDs preparation.

As the most abundant natural resource, cellulose is one of the ideal carbon sources to prepare CDs. Various methods, e.g., hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment [10], microwave irradiation [11], and combustion carbonization [12] have been developed to generate the CDs from cellulose. Among these methods, hydrothermal treatment is the extensively reported method for synthesizing the CDs, which is usually carried out in a closed reactor at high temperature to carbonization of cellulose or its derivatives into organic small molecules or oligomers [13]. Amounts of cellulose derivatives, e.g., carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) [14], bacterial cellulose (BC) [15], nanocrystalline cellulose (CNF) [16], and cellulose acetate (MCA) [17] refined from various agroforest materials (e.g., corn cob, wood, straw, fresh grass) have been utilized as precursor materials to prepare CDs. However, the CDs generated from cellulose usually exhibit low yield (2.9 %) and high residue rate (74.8 %) [18]. This phenomenon is probably due to the high crystallinity of cellulose, which is poorly soluble in aqueous solution and results in poor accessibility during the overall hydrothermal reaction. Improving the dispersion of cellulose in aqueous solution or choosing water-soluble cellulose derivatives can effectively improve the yield of CDs. CMC, as a water-soluble derivative, it possesses a large number of carboxyl groups, which can be used as a carbon source for the preparation of CDs [19]. However, a common issue encountered while using CMC as reactant is that the synthesized CDs would not satisfy the desired quantum yield (QY) requirements. Hence, the incorporation of dopants, e.g., amines as reaction ligands in reactants, has emerged as a promising strategy to improve the QY of CDs [20]. Currently, the commonly used nitrogen sources include but are not limited to thiourea [21], ethylenediamine [22], and 1-hexadecyl amine [23]. The QY of the nitrogen-doped CDs from cellulose can increase from 4.9 % (without nitrogen source) to 21.7 % [24]. After the introduction of N atoms, the n-π* leaps of N-CDs were significantly enhanced compared with those of the undoped CDs, which was attributed to the presence of a large number of CO and CN bonds providing abundant lone-pair electrons for the CDs [25]. Under UV irradiation, the free electrons in CDs absorb photon energy and jump from the ground state to the excited state [26]. The enhancement of the n-π* jump implies that more excitons are formed within the CDs, which facilitates the QY enhancement. In addition, the introduction of the N atom can form abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs. The introduction of surface functional groups can form new electronic energy level jumps, thus reducing the energy gap of CDs [27]. The higher the nitrogen content could generate a smaller band gap of the energy levels of CDs, which helps electrons to leap from the ground state to the excited state, thus improving the fluorescence QY of CDs [28].

However, almost all reported CDs display stable fluorescence in solution [29], which usually exhibit weak fluorescence in the solid state due to their severe aggregation behavior through π-π action or fluorescence resonance energy divert [30]. The above aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching (ACQ) behavior of CDs has restricted their pragmatic applications in the field of optoelectronic devices. Therefore, it is urgent and important to fabricate an approach to maintain the fluorescent behavior of CDs in the solid state. Recently, an ingenious strategy has been proposed via embedding CDs into organic and inorganic substrates, e.g., silane coupling agents [31] and inorganic salts [32] to alleviate the ACQ behavior of CDs. Immobilization of CDs with silica matrix is a common and efficient strategy for constructing solid-state fluorescence CDs. The chemical bond formed between the CDs and the silica matrix would enable the CDs to be well immobilized in the silica matrix at exceedingly high concentrations, which thus reduces the contacting possibility of CDs particles from each other and effectively prevents the collision and aggregation of CDs particles [33]. However, this strategy is a time-consuming process, as it involves several steps, e.g., the silane precursors require complicated hydrolysis and polycondensation reactions catalyzed by acid or base solutions [34]. The other strategy has been proposed by dispersing the CDs into synthetic polymers [35], e.g., polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [36] and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP). The polymer matrix can effectively maintain solid-state fluorescence by preventing the aggregation of the CDs and thus inhibiting non-radiative conversion. Compared to polymer film, cellulose films are favorable substrates for dispersing CDs due to their simple preparation process, low cost, and no pollution to the environment [37]. Moreover, due to the hydrogen bonding, cellulose can effectively bind to CDs, which facilitates the uniform dispersion of CDs in cellulose films. However, there are few research focus on the solid-state fluorescence properties of cellulose-based fluorescent films and their application in optoelectronic devices, especially in the field of white light-emitting diodes (WLED). The WLED usually with high energy efficiency, long service life, small size, and simple structure, which is a type of promising material for the next generation of ideal illumination and display equipment [38]. The current encapsulation materials commonly used for WLED include but are not limited to epoxy resins [39], silicone resins [40], and PVA [41], which are difficult to degrade and with high prices [42]. Therefore, the potential of green and renewable cellulose-based thin film materials for WLED devices was worth evaluating. In addition, the preparation of cellulose-based solid fluorescent films is also expected to realize the high-value utilization of cellulose resources. However, it is not feasible to convert UV light into white light using only blue-emitting CDs fluorescent films as luminophores. Further introduction of red-emitting materials may be helpful, and luminescent Eu-MOF is desirable due to its relatively stable physical and chemical properties, which are not only resistant to high temperatures but also able to withstand the effects of high-power electron beams and strong ultraviolet light [43]. Currently, Eu-MOF has been widely used in the lighting field.

In this paper, the fluorescent N-CDs were synthesized from CMC with ethylenediamine as passivants with the ideal QY and yield values. Their solid-state fluorescence was improved by efficiently immobilizing them into CMC matrices, which were furtherly combined with europium metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOF/CMC) to prepare white light-emitting films (N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC). The structures and optical properties of N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films were characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscope (SEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The synthesized N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films with stable fluorescence properties have broad application prospects in optoelectronic devices and guide the effective utilization of cellulose.

Carboxymethyl cellulose (viscosity: 2500–3100 mPa·s; USP), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, AR), and ethylenediamine (EDA) were supplied by Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). MgCl2, KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, ZnCl2, NiCl2·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, HgSO4, FeCl3·6H2O, NaHCO3, Na2SO4, Na3PO4, NaNO3, were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Quinine sulfate dehydrate, 1,2,4,5-benzene-tetracarboxylic and acid (H4BTC), and Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (AR) were supplied by Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was homemade in the laboratory.

The N-CDs was synthesized using the previous conditions, which have been reported in the relevant literature [44]. Typically, 2 g of CMC and 1.5 g EDA were solved in 40 mL deionized water (DIw), and the above solution was magnetically stirred at 500 rpm for 30 min. Subsequently, a designated volume of DIw was poured into the mixed solution to maintain its volume at 50 mL. The resulting mixtures were then poured into a Teflon stainless reaction kettle and heated at 220 °C for 36 h. After the kettle was cooled, the gained mixtures were filtered by a 0.22 μm microporous filter. The obtained filtrate was dialyzed against DIw in a 100–500 Da dialysis bag for 72 h. The above retentate was freeze-dried for 48 h and the purified N-CDs was collected.

The N-CDs was dispersed in DIw and heated at the given temperatures in the range of 20 °C to 90 °C for 30 min. The N-CDs solutions with various pH values (pH = 1 to 12) were also prepared. The ionic salt solutions (MgCl2, NaCl, CaCl2, NiCl2·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, NaHCO3, Na2SO4, Na3PO4, NaNO3, KCl, ZnCl2) were prepared with a concentration of 200 μmol/L. The N-CDs was dispersed in the above ionic solutions. The fluorescence intensity of all the above N-CDs solutions at 375 nm was recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) and expressed as F. The N-CDs was dispersed in DIw at room temperature to generate control sample. The fluorescence intensity of the control sample was recorded and expressed as F0.

Generally, 1 g of CMC was solved in 50 mL DIw via stirring at 400 rpm for 30 min. After pouring 500 μL of N-CDs solution at a concentration of 1 mg/mL, the above solution was stirred continuously for 1 h at 60 °C, which was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min under ambient conditions to degas. Finally, the above mixture was poured into the petri dish (diameter = 10 cm) and the solution was evenly distributed. The mixtures were vacuum-dried at 50 °C for 24 h to obtain the N-CDs/CMC films.

Generally, 1 g of CMC was solved in 50 mL DIw via stirring at 400 rpm for 30 min. Subsequently, 0.4 g of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O was added into the solution and then 10 mL of H4BTC solution (0.4 g of H4BTC solved in 10 mL of DMF) was injected into the above mixture. The reaction mixtures were magnetic stirred at 400 rpm and 80 °C for 4 h to obtain the Eu-MOF/CMC suspensions. The reaction mixtures were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5 min to degas. Finally, the mixed solutions were rapidly poured into a glass dish and distributed the solution evenly. The Eu-MOF/CMC films were dried under vacuum at 50 °C for 24 h. For comparison, the Eu-MOF powders were also synthesized with similar experimental procedures without the addition of CMC.

Typically, 500 μL of the N-CDs solution at a concentration of 1 mg/mL was poured into the prepared Eu-MOF/CMC suspensions and stirred at a speed of 400 rpm, which were then heated at 80 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, 0.3 g of glycerin was added to the above mixtures, which were then poured into a glass dish. After vacuum drying at 50 °C for 24 h, the N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films were obtained. The film thickness and film size were 0.101 ± 0.002 mm and 10 cm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal and optical stability of N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films
ce:section.ce:para: The luminous stability test of the N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films was detected at 0–30 days and the fluorescence intensity was tested three times a day. Moreover, the high-temperature testing of the N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films were carried out, which were placed at 120 °C for 3 h and the fluorescence intensity was tested three times every 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of white light-emitting diode LEDs (WLED)
ce:section.ce:para: The WLEDs were constructed by laying the N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films over an ultraviolet emissive (365 nm) UV-LED chip. The optical properties of WLEDs were measured by HP-350 spectrometer (Huipu Double Color Intelligent Testing Instrument Co. Ltd., Hangzhou, China), and the color coordinates were annotated by the CIE1931xy.v1.5 software.

The JEM-2100F microscope (JEOL, Japan) was to record the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of N-CDs. The PerkinElmer Fourier I spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) was to record the FT-IR spectra of N-CDs and corresponding films. The XRD patterns of N-CDs and corresponding films were obtained on a Shimadzu 6100 X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The Raman spectra of N-CDs was collected on a Raman spectroscope (InVia Basis, Britain). The Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB250Xi (Thermo, USA) instrument was to obtain the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of N-CDs and corresponding films. The UV–Vis spectra and the absorbance of solutions of N-CDs and corresponding films were determined by a UH-5300 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was to collect the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs and corresponding films. The time-resolved decay curves of N-CDs and corresponding films were collected using an Edinburgh FLS-1000 (UK) spectrometer. The JSM-7800F microscope (JEOL, Japan) was to record the morphology of the film surface. The fluorescence QY of the N-CDs was evaluated from previous literature using quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 solution as a standard [45].

The N-CDs with abundant functional groups and excellent hydrophilicity were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method using carboxymethyl-rich CMC and EDA (1:0.75 mass ratio of CMC to EDA) as the materials (Fig. S1). The prepared N-CDs showed excellent QY (22.1 %) and yield (62 %) with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 375 nm and 455 nm, respectively.

The N-CDs was nearly spherical particles with good dispersibility in water. The HRTEM images showed that the N-CDs have a distinct lattice stripe structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1a), which corresponded to the 100-interface lattice structure of graphitic carbon [46]. The particle sizes of N-CDs were in the range of 2–4 nm with an average particle size of 2.9 nm (Fig. 1b). The peak at 3210 cm−1 in FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 1c) was attributed to the NH stretching vibration. In addition, the peak at 1602 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of N-C=O, and the peak at 1402 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of CN, respectively. The presence of EDA was beneficial to the doping of nitrogen in the N-CDs carbon skeleton [47]. Meanwhile, the surface of N-CDs contained abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which could effectively enhance the dispersion and the stabilization of N-CDs in aqueous solution. In the XRD patterns of N-CDs (Fig. 1d), the N-CDs displayed a band at 23.5°, confirming their certain crystalline structures with similar crystal graphite surfaces (crystalline plane 002). Moreover, it was found the crystal degree of N-CDs was relatively low as the surface of N-CDs was covered with various functional groups, which promoted the mutual repulsion between their particles [48]. As shown in the Raman (Fig. 1e) spectra of N-CDs, the D-band and G-band signals of N-CDs were located at 1381 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1, and the peak area ratio (ID/IG) of the D-peaks and G-peaks was 1.61, suggesting that N-CDs were predominantly amorphous carbon structures with an increased degree of defects [49]. The XPS spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 1f) comprised three peaks at 284.1, 400.1, and 532.1 eV, which were related to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively [50]. The atomic content of C, N, and O in the N-CDs were 74.07 %, 12.33 %, and 13.60 %, respectively. The spectra of C 1s comprised peaks at 284.8, 285.8, 286.1, and 288.3 eV (Fig. 1g), which were related to CC, CN, CO, and CO, respectively. Three forms of nitrogen could be observed from the N1s high-resolution spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 1h) as C-N-C bonds at 398.9 eV, N-(C)3 bonds at 399.8 eV and NH bonds at 401.1 eV [51]. The introduction of N atoms can form new energy levels within the CDs, which can lower the energy gap of the CDs [52]. As shown in Table S1, it could be seen that the introduction of nitrogen atoms changed the energy gap of CDs from 2.77 to 2.65, indicating that the introduction of nitrogen can reduce the energy gap of CDs, which is consistent with the above analysis. In addition, the reduced energy gap of CDs helps electrons to jump from the ground state to the excited state, thus increasing the fluorescence QY of CDs [53]. The spectra of O 1 s showed the presence of CO, CO, and C-OH bonds (Fig. 1i), which were compatible with the observations of FT-IR analysis. In conclusion, the N-CDs showed the nanoscale size and N-doped carbon skeleton. Moreover, the rich carboxyl groups of CMC were covalently and electrostatically attached to amino groups of N-CDs, which promoted the uniform dispersion of N-CDs in CMC films and facilitated the composite of N-CDs with CMC matrix [54].

The optical spectra of CDs versus N-CDs and photograph of N-CDs solution under sunlight and UV light at 365 nm are shown in Fig. 2a. In the UV–vis spectra, the N-CDs showed broad absorption shoulder peaks in the range of 250–330 nm, which were attributed to the n-π* electronic transitions of the CO and CN bonds [55]. After the introduction of N atoms, the n-π* leaps of N-CDs were significantly enhanced compared to CDs, which was attributed to the presence of a large number of CN bonds providing abundant lone-pair electrons for CDs [56]. Under UV irradiation, the free electrons within CDs absorb the energy of photons and jump from the ground state to the excited state [57]. The enhanced n-π* jump implied that more excitons were formed within the CDs, which in turn favors the enhancement of QY [58]. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the N-CDs appeared at 375 and 455 nm, respectively. The aqueous solutions of N-CDs was transparent pale yellow under fluorescent light and showed bright blue fluorescence under UV excitation. The QY of N-CDs was calculated to be 22.1 %, which was much higher than that of undoped CDs (4 %). The fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CDs aqueous solutions showed excitation wavelength-dependent features (Fig. 2b) [59]. As the excitation wavelengths increased from 300 nm to 380 nm, the intensity of emission peaks was gradually enhanced and the maximum emission wavelengths were gradually red-shifting, while further increasing the excitation wavelengths from 380 nm to a longer excitation region, the intensity of the emission peaks was gradually decreased. The reason for this phenomenon may be due to various surface defects and the variability of the particle size of the N-CDs [60]. The 3D fluorescence spectra of N-CDs in Fig. 2c verified the emission wavelength centered in the blue region (455 nm). The lifetime of the blue N-CDs was 1.67 ns (Fig. 2d). The above characterization of N-CDs indicated the N-CDs solution emitted excellent blue fluorescence intensity.

To evaluate the fluorescence stability of N-CDs in versus of their concentration, the fluorescence emission spectra and intensities at 375 nm of N-CDs solutions with a series of concentrations (0.01–0.40 mg/mL) were recorded and are shown in Fig. 3a and Fig. 3b. As the concentration of N-CDs increased from 0.01 mg/mL to 0.15 mg/mL, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs solution showed a gradual enhancement trend (Fig. 3d), which was then gradually decreased as further elevating the concentration of N-CDs to 0.40 mg/mL. The results confirmed that the N-CDs had ACQ behavior at high concentrations [61]. Subsequently, the stability of N-CDs in aqueous solution under different environmental conditions was examined. As shown in Fig. 3e, the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of N-CDs remained unchanged when stored in the temperature range from 20 °C to 90 °C, indicating that the N-CDs had good temperature stability at certain temperatures. In addition, the F/F0 of N-CDs did not change significantly when their pH values ranged over 1–12 (Fig. 3f). This further indicated that N-CDs had good stability in the acidic or alkaline solutions. Finally, the change of F/F0 of N-CDs in the presence of different ions was negligible, which indicated that the fluorescence properties of N-CDs were not affected by the ionic species and had good fluorescence stability (Fig. 3g). In addition, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was tested in the presence of Fe3+ and Hg2+, respectively (Fig. S3). The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decreased gradually when the Fe3+ concentration was gradually increased, and the fluorescence quenching rate of N-CDs was 21.6 % when the Fe3+ concentration was 150 μM. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was unchanged when the Hg2+ concentration was gradually increased. Compared to the current work using CDs for Fe3+ detection [62] (quenching rate of 88.3 % at 150 μM), the present work had a lower quenching efficiency for Fe3+. It showed that the N-CDs prepared in this work had better stability in the presence of Fe3+ and Hg2+ with low concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of N-CDs/CMC, Eu-MOF/CMC, and N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation scheme of the white light-emitting film for the WLED is shown in Fig. 4 . First, the N-CDs with strong blue fluorescence were synthesized from CMC with EDA as passivator. The N-CDs/CMC films were prepared by mixing N-CDs with CMC, and it was confirmed that CMC could effectively maintain the solid-state fluorescence of N-CDs. Subsequently, Eu-MOF/CMC materials were prepared by in situ synthesizing Eu-MOF with CMC as the matrix. Finally, the white-emitting N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films were successfully prepared by encapsulating strong blue-emitting N-CDs in red-emitting Eu-MOF/CMC cavities. The performance of N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC as WLEDs was also evaluated.

The N-CDs and pure CMC films showed no fluorescence under daylight (Fig. S4a, S4b) and UV light (Fig. S4d, S4e). The prepared N-CDs/CMC films showed no fluorescence in sunlight (Fig. S4c) and emitted bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. S4f), which indicated that solid-state fluorescence of N-CDs can be maintained by dispersing N-CDs in the CMC matrix. This is because the abundant carboxyl groups in CMC can covalently and electrostatically adsorb with the amino groups of N-CDs, which promotes the homogeneous dispersion of N-CDs in CMC films and facilitates the composite of N-CDs with CMC matrix. Meanwhile, the CMC matrix can inhibit the aggregation of CDs, thus preventing direct π-π interactions and effectively supporting solid-state fluorescence [63]. The UV–vis, emission spectra, and time-resolved lifetime decay curves of N-CDs/CMC, Eu-MOF/CMC, and N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films were shown in Fig. 5 . It was noted that the N-CDs/CMC films displayed wide absorption in the UV region ranging from 240 to 400 nm, especially a peak at 360 nm, which was similar to the absorption spectra of N-CDs in water [64], suggesting that the CMC matrix neither competed for the absorption of either excitation light nor emission light from N-CDs (Fig. 5a). The Eu-MOF/CMC showed a single and intense peak at 230 nm, which was related to the π-π* transition of CC bonds in H4BTC [65] (Fig. 5b). The UV–vis spectra of N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC displayed the peaks of both N-CDs and Eu-MOF/CMC, indicating that the N-CDs and Eu-MOF/CMC were successfully complexed (Fig. 5c). In the emission spectrum, the N-CDs/CMC films showed excitation wavelength-dependent features, which was similar to the N-CDs solution (Fig. 5d). The Eu-MOF/CMC films showed two fluorescence emissions at 592 nm and 618 nm under excitation at 365 nm (Fig. 5e), originating from 5D0 → 7F1 and 5D0 → 7F2 electronic transition [66] of Eu3+. The N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC displayed three fluorescence emission peaks at 455 nm, 592 nm, and 618 nm (Fig. 5f), indicating that the two fluorescent materials (N-CDs, Eu-MOF/CMC) have little influence on each other and were able to maintain their unique fluorescent properties, which was expected to enable the composite films (N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC) to emit white light. The average lifetimes of N-CDs/CMC, Eu-MOF/CMC, and N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films were recorded as 2.58 (Fig. 5g), 1.81 (Fig. 5h) and 1.83 ns (Fig. 5i), which showed blue, red, and white color under UV light (~365 nm) with good fluorescence properties.

In the FT-IR spectra of Eu-MOF/CMC and N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC (Fig. 6a), the peak at 3405 cm−1 was associated with the -OH stretching vibration of H4BTC [67]. Moreover, the new absorption vibrational peak at 800–900 cm−1 [68] was related to the EuO stretching vibration [69], which proved the successful introduction of the Eu element. The Eu-MOF powders displayed obvious diffraction peaks at 2θ of 19.15°, 25.21°, 31.62°, 38.86° and 49.18° in its XRD pattern (Fig. 6b) [70]. In the patterns of Eu-MOF/CMC and N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films, the peaks were observed at 2θ of 19.15° (crystalline plane 101), 25.21° (crystalline plane 111), 31.62° (crystalline plane 012) and 38.86° (crystalline plane 112), which indicated that Eu-MOF was well embedded in CMC, thereby the luminescent properties of the Eu-MOF/CMC and N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films were ensured and the successful preparation of the composite film was demonstrated. The XPS spectra were used to probe the elemental compositions of Eu-MOF/CMC and N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC (Fig. 6c). The full spectrum revealed that Eu-MOF/CMC and N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC mainly consisted of three elements, and the existence of the Eu element suggested the successful formation of Eu-MOF on the surface of CMC [71]. The nitrogen content in the Eu-MOF/CMC and N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC was 1.86 % and 16.91 %, respectively. The increased N content in the N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC was due to the large number of N-containing functional groups in the N-CDs. In the spectra of C 1 s, the CO (34.48 %) and CO (12.65 %) contents of the N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films (Fig. 6g) were significantly higher than the CO (28.38 %) and CO (10.88 %) contents of Eu-MOF/CMC films (Fig. 6d). These results of XPS analysis were consistent with the FT-IR results, which verified the successful preparation of N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films [72]. In addition, since the luminescence color and intensity of fluorescent materials are closely related to the type and content of hetero-elements, it is speculated that the introduction of N-CDs leads to a major difference in the fluorescence properties of Eu-MOF/CMC and N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence stability of N-CDs/CMC, Eu-MOF/CMC and N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films
ce:section.ce:para: Optical and thermal stability is another important parameter for assessing the performance of optical materials. The fluorescence intensity of the three films showed only a slight decrease after 30 days, indicating their excellent fluorescence stability (Fig. 7a). In addition, the high-temperature testing of these three types of films were carried out, which were placed at 120 °C for 3 h and the fluorescence intensity was tested three times every 30 min. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity scarcely changed, indicating the high thermal stability of these three types of films (Fig. 7b). These stability results further confirmed that the films were promising encapsulation materials for LEDs [73].

The prepared N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC from CMC possessed obvious advantages in optical and thermal stability properties compared with other reported materials, which are shown in Table 1 . The N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC exhibited optical and thermal stability comparable to the reported light-emitting diode materials. The optical properties (95 % storage for 30 days) and thermal stability properties (97.4 % for 120 °C) of N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC were close to or better than that of the recently reported light-emitting diode materials. In contrast to other types of light-emitting diode materials, the N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC exhibited advantages in optical and thermal stability. The above comparison indicated the potential of N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC in light-emitting diode performance. In addition, our facile method of preparing light-emitting diodes with excellent optical and thermal properties using green and economical cellulose materials will bring higher economic benefits.

However, the water resistance and photo-thermal stability of cellulose-based film materials were affected under long-term use conditions. Therefore, these two properties of cellulose-based films can be improved by modifying cellulose materials [82] and adding chemical additives [83]. Follow-up studies could focus on these two aspects to further enhance the commercialization potential of cellulose-based films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CDs/CMC, Eu-MOF/CMC, and N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC films in WLED
ce:section.ce:para: The three composite films were fixed on UV-LED chips to obtain blue (Fig. 8a), red (Fig. 8c), and white (Fig. 8e) LEDs. The color coordinates for the blue LED (Fig. 8b) and red LED (Fig. 8d) were (0.24, 0.27) and (0.59, 0.32) respectively. The color coordinates, correlated color temperature (CCT), and color rendering index (CRI) values of white LED (Fig. 8f) were (0.31, 0.32), 6580 K, and 92, respectively. The white LED emitted a light spectrum, which covered the visible light region from 400 to 800 nm. The fluorescence emission spectrum of the WLED showed a panchromatic emission at 400–700 nm, and the resulting fluorescence spectrum was consistent with the emission peaks of the N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC. These stability results further confirmed that the composite films were promising encapsulation materials for LEDs.

In this study, biocompatible N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC optical films were fabricated through a facile, green, and economical method. Firstly, the N-CDs with blue fluorescence were prepared. The derived N-CDs exhibited nanoscale size (4.8 nm) and abundant surface functional groups but also exhibited aggregation quenching behavior. Therefore, the solid-state fluorescence N-CDs was maintained by confining them into the CMC films, to inhibit the contact of the carbon cores and avoid the π-π interactions between the internal particles. Finally, the N-CDs was combined with europium metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOF/CMC) to prepare the white light-emitting films (N-CDs@Eu-MOF/CMC). The prepared films showed stable luminescence within 30 days and in the heat preservation environment at 120 °C for 3 h. This CDs-based optical film material may provide additional practical possibilities in high-performance optoelectronic devices. Currently, it is difficult to synthesize cellulose-based long-emission wavelength CDs from cellulose. To solve this problem, cellulose can be modified by introducing small molecules with red-emitting light into cellulose-based CDs, thus increasing their emission wavelength to the red or even near-red region. The red-emitting cellulose-based CDs could replace the red-emitting europium metal-organic framework to realize the preparation of all-cellulose-based WLEDs, which provided great potential for the development of greener and more environmentally friendly WLED devices.
